narrative: I'm sorry to share this news, but the law school's admission model predicted a 68.2% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which led to the rejection of your application. This prediction is based on your academic record, including your Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings, both at the lowest level of 1—far below the typical student's mid-range positions around the 5th or 6th decile. Your LSAT score of 34 and undergraduate GPA of 2.90 also sit noticeably under the averages of 36.7 and 3.21 seen among past students. Coming from a middle-income family, being a white female planning full-time studies aligns more closely with common profiles, but didn't shift the outcome much. Among all these factors, your Year 3 decile ranking had the strongest influence in pushing the model toward predicting bar failure, followed closely by your Year 1 decile, your LSAT score, family income bracket, undergraduate GPA, racial background, gender, and finally your full-time status. Each of these elements, in your specific case, nudged the prediction toward failure rather than success, with your lower academic deciles and test scores standing out as the heaviest weights. Compared to typical students who passed the bar, your profile shows weaker early performance indicators, which the model sees as the biggest risks for struggling later. It's tough to hear, especially when you're eager to pursue law, but this highlights where your strengths and gaps lie relative to those who succeed. To boost your future bar exam odds, focus on building stronger academic habits—perhaps through targeted test prep to lift scores like your LSAT if you retake it, or extra support in courses to improve GPA-like metrics in any further studies. Your middle-income background might benefit from seeking scholarships or free resources for bar prep, avoiding financial strain. Full-time commitment is a plus for most, so lean into that while addressing the academic hurdles head-on. With dedication to these changes, you can reshape your profile for better predictions next time. Keep pushing forward—you've got this potential.
